etymology.description: Top resembling a ghost head 鬼(鬼) (guǐ, "ghost") or mask 異(異) (yì, "different") + bottom resembling 禹(禹) (yǔ, "a legendary monarch") or trample 禸(禸) (rǒu, "to trample"); considered a pictogram of a kind of insect
etymology.interpretation: The character's top was initially thought to represent a ghost's head or a ceremonial mask, while the bottom section might signify actions such as trampling. Some scholars see the earlier forms of 禺 resembling an insect, possibly with decorative features, drawing a parallel to the mark found in 萬(萬) (wàn, "scorpion"). Over time, it became associated with a type of ape, likely due to folk associations with its appearance.
